perturbation.original_text: You agree to be responsible for any failure by your Representatives to comply with this Agreement (including, without limitation, any actions or inactions by your Representatives that would constitute a breach if such Representatives were original signatories hereto (other than with respect to paragraph 7 below under the heading “Non-Solicitation and Non-Hire of Employees”)).
perturbation.changed_text: You agree to be responsible for any failure by your Representatives to comply with this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the parenthetical clarifies that the firm is responsible for *all* failures by its Representatives, including non-solicitation and non-hire, which is a restriction of the rights of employees. This is non-compliant, because some states ban Non-compete agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The modified clause, by removing the exception for paragraph 7 (Non-Solicitation and Non-Hire of Employees), now implies a broader restriction on Representatives, potentially conflicting with California's prohibition on non-compete agreements, especially regarding employees who might leave and start competing businesses.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

perturbation.justification: The original contract text explicitly excludes paragraph 7, concerning "Non-Solicitation and Non-Hire of Employees," from the firm's responsibility for its representatives' failures. The changed text removes this exclusion, making the firm responsible for *all* failures, including those related to non-solicitation and non-hire. According to the Law Explanation, this broadened responsibility could be seen as a non-compete agreement, which is prohibited by California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The removal of the exception now places a broader restriction on Representatives, potentially conflicting with California law.

perturbation.original_text: If you or your Representatives are requested or required to disclose any Evaluation Material or any of the information referred to in paragraph 4 above under the heading “Non-Disclosure of Transaction Discussions” (or if the Company or its Representatives is requested or required to disclose that you are in discussions or negotiations with the Company concerning a possible Transaction) pursuant to any law or regulation or stock exchange rule or regulation or the terms of a valid and effective subpoena or order issued by a court of competent jurisdiction or a federal, state or local governmental or regulatory body or pursuant to a civil investigative demand or similar judicial process or otherwise, the applicable party will, to the extent not prohibited by applicable law or regulation, (i) promptly notify the other party of the existence, terms and circumstances surrounding such request or requirement, (ii) consult with the other party on the advisability of taking legally available steps to resist or narrow such request or requirement, (iii) if disclosure of any such information is required, disclose only that portion of the information which it is legally required to disclose and give the other party notice of the information to be so disclosed as far in advance of disclosure as may be reasonably practicable and (iv) except in the case of public disclosure with respect to any discussions or negotiations concerning a possible Transaction which is required by reason of securities laws or similar requirements related to general disclosure, if so requested by the other party, exercise its commercially reasonable efforts to obtain a protective order or other reliable assurance that confidential treatment will be accorded to such information (and, in any event, if applicable, such party will reasonably cooperate with the other party to obtain such a protective order or other assurance), such efforts to obtain a protective order or confidential treatment to be at the other (requesting) party’s expense.
perturbation.changed_text: If you or your Representatives are requested or required to disclose any Evaluation Material or any of the information referred to in paragraph 4 above under the heading “Non-Disclosure of Transaction Discussions” (or if the Company or its Representatives is requested or required to disclose that you are in discussions or negotiations with the Company concerning a possible Transaction) pursuant to any law or regulation or stock exchange rule or regulation or the terms of a valid and effective subpoena or order issued by a court of competent jurisdiction or a federal, state or local governmental or regulatory body or pursuant to a civil investigative demand or similar judicial process or otherwise, the applicable party will, (iii) if disclosure of any such information is required, disclose only that portion of the information which it is legally required to disclose.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement to notify the other party and seek a protective order, the modified clause allows for unilateral disclosure of confidential information without consulting the other party. This could conflict with laws protecting trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) protects trade secrets. While it allows for disclosure under court order, it generally expects reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Removing the requirement to notify the other party and seek a protective order weakens these protections and increases the risk of trade secret misappropriation, which is a violation of 18 U.S.C. § 1836.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes critical obligations that could potentially undermine trade secret protection, conflicting with the spirit of the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), as described in the 'Law Explanation'. Specifically, the original text required the disclosing party to notify the other party and seek a protective order. By omitting these obligations, the changed text weakens efforts to maintain secrecy, increasing the risk of trade secret misappropriation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 18 U.S.C. § 1836, which the Law Explanation suggests is relevant to the DTSA's protection of trade secrets. Even though Scraped Law Snippet 2 is unavailable, the information in Scraped Law Snippet 1, combined with the explanation that removing the notification and protective order requirements weakens trade secret protections is enough to conclude that the omission creates a significant legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: You acknowledge that money damages and remedies at law may be inadequate to protect the Company against any actual or threatened breach of or failure to comply with this Agreement by you or by your Representatives and, without prejudice to any rights and remedies otherwise available to the Company, you may agree to the seeking of specific performance, injunctive relief and other equitable remedies in the Company’s favor, and you further agree to waive, and to cause your Representatives to waive, any requirement for the securing or posting of any bond in connection with any such remedy.
perturbation.changed_text: You acknowledge that money damages and remedies at law may be inadequate to protect the Company against any actual or threatened breach of or failure to comply with this Agreement by you or by your Representatives and, without prejudice to any rights and remedies otherwise available to the Company, you may agree to the seeking of specific performance, injunctive relief and other equitable remedies in the Company’s favor.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of the clause where the Representatives waive the bond in connection with any equitable remedy infringes on the right to due process.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Fifth Amendment of the US Constitution protects against deprivation of property without due process of law. While the right to require a bond isn't explicitly mentioned, the bond serves as protection for a party that is unfairly injuncted (i.e., their property is deprived). The bond ensures that if the injunction turns out to be wrong, the other party is compensated for the damages. Eliminating the requirement to post a bond prior to injunction could violate due process rights if that protection is removed. By forcing a party to waive bond requirements for injunctive relief, the terms of the contract potentially contradict constitutional due process rights by limiting a defendant's ability to recover damages resulting from an improperly granted injunction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Fifth Amendment | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Fifth Amendment The Fifth Amendment creates a number of rights relevant to both criminal and civil legal proceedings. In criminal cases , the Fifth Amendment guarantees the right to a grand jury , forbids “ double jeopardy ,” and protects against self-incrimination . It also requires that “ due process of law ” be part of any proceeding that denies a citizen “life, liberty or property” and requires the government to compensate citizens when it takes private property for public use. Learn more... Primary tabs Amendment V No person shall be held to answer for a capital, or otherwise infamous crime, unless on a presentment or indictment of a grand jury, except in cases arising in the land or naval forces, or in the militia, when in actual service in time of war or public danger; nor shall any person be subject for the same offense to be twice put in jeopardy of life or limb; nor shall be compelled in any criminal case to be a witness against himself, nor be deprived of life, liberty, or property, without due process of law; nor shall private property be taken for public use, without just compensation. Wex Resources Fifth Amendment Criminal Law / Criminal Procedure Due Process Substantive Due Process Miranda Warning Indictment Privilege Against Self-Incrimination Self-Incrimination Grand Jury Jury Double Jeopardy Collateral Estoppel Immunity From Prosecution Eminent Domain Takings Territorial Jurisdiction Other Resources US Code: Title 18 Federal Rules of Criminal Procedure ‹ Fourth Amendment up Sixth Amendment › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.justification: The change contradicts due process as the altered text removes the requirement for the representatives to waive the bond related to equitable remedies, potentially infringing on due process rights. The Law Explanation states that eliminating the bond requirement could violate due process rights. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referring to the Fifth Amendment, states that no person shall “be deprived of life, liberty, or property, without due process of law”. By removing the bond requirement, the altered text potentially allows for deprivation of property without the protection afforded by the bond, which is intended to compensate for damages resulting from an improperly granted injunction, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The validity and interpretation of this Agreement will be governed by, and construed and enforced in accordance with, the laws of the State of New York, without giving effect to conflicts of laws principles. You irrevocably (i) submit to the jurisdiction of any court of the State of New York located in New York, New York or the United States District Court for the Southern District of New York for the purpose of any suit, action, or other proceeding arising out of this Agreement, or any of the transactions contemplated hereby (each a “Proceeding”) and agree that service of any process, summons, notice or document delivered by hand or sent by U.S. registered mail to your address set forth above will be effective service of process for any action, suit or proceeding brought against you in any such court, (ii) agree that all claims in respect of any Proceeding may be heard and determined in any such court, (iii) irrevocably waive, to the fullest extent permitted by law, any immunity you have acquired, or hereafter may acquire, from jurisdiction of any such court or from any legal process therein, (iv) irrevocably waive, to the fullest extent permitted by law, any right to trial by jury in any Proceeding and (v) agree not to commence any Proceeding other than in such court, and waive, to the fullest extent permitted by applicable law, any claim that any such Proceeding is brought in an inconvenient forum.
perturbation.changed_text: The validity and interpretation of this Agreement will be governed by, and construed and enforced in accordance with, the laws of the State of New York, without giving effect to conflicts of laws principles. You irrevocably (i) submit to the jurisdiction of any court of the State of New York located in New York, New York or the United States District Court for the Southern District of New York for the purpose of any suit, action, or other proceeding arising out of this Agreement, or any of the transactions contemplated hereby (each a “Proceeding”) and agree that service of any process, summons, notice or document delivered by hand or sent by U.S. registered mail to your address set forth above will be effective service of process for any action, suit or proceeding brought against you in any such court, (ii) agree that all claims in respect of any Proceeding may be heard and determined in any such court, (iii) irrevocably waive, to the fullest extent permitted by law, any immunity you have acquired, or hereafter may acquire, from jurisdiction of any such court or from any legal process therein, (v) agree not to commence any Proceeding other than in such court, and waive, to the fullest extent permitted by applicable law, any claim that any such Proceeding is brought in an inconvenient forum.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause stating that the parties irrevocably waive their right to a jury trial, it is possible to contradict the 7th amendment in the US Constitution.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Seventh Amendment to the United States Constitution guarantees the right to a jury trial in civil cases where the amount in controversy exceeds $20. While this right can be waived, removing the explicit waiver clause in the contract opens the possibility for a party to demand a jury trial, potentially conflicting with the intent of the original agreement. This amendment is about the right to a jury trial, and by removing the waiver it introduces a legal risk.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Seventh Amendment | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Seventh Amendment The Seventh Amendment continues a practice from English common law of distinguishing civil claims which must be tried before a jury (absent waiver by the parties) from claims and issues that may be heard by a judge alone. It only governs federal civil courts and has no application to civil courts set up by the states when those courts are hearing only disputes of state law . Learn more... Primary tabs Amendment VII In suits at common law, where the value in controversy shall exceed twenty dollars, the right of trial by jury shall be preserved, and no fact tried by a jury, shall be otherwise reexamined in any court of the United States, than according to the rules of the common law. Wex Resources Criminal Law Criminal Procedure Jury Other Resources US Code: Title 18 Federal Rules of Criminal Procedure ‹ Sixth Amendment up Eighth Amendment › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.justification: The modified contract omits the explicit waiver of the right to a jury trial, potentially contradicting the Seventh Amendment, which preserves that right unless waived. The provided law snippet confirms that the Seventh Amendment distinguishes civil claims tried by a jury from those heard by a judge, 'absent waiver by the parties'. By removing the waiver, the contract now allows a party to demand a jury trial, which introduces a legal risk conflicting with the initial intent to prevent jury trials.
